questions.question: Explain the concept of DFD balancing between different levels. If a Level-1 DFD has a data flow 'Customer Order' entering Process P1 and leaving as 'Order Details', and the parent Level-0 (context diagram) shows 'Order Information' entering the system, explain how balancing is achieved and what transformations occur at Level-1.
keyPoints: Parent process at Level-0 must balance with child processes at Level-1
All input/output data flows from parent must be preserved in children
Decomposition adds internal detail without changing external interface
Data flow 'Customer Order' is decomposed into 'Order Details' plus possibly other flows like 'Order Confirmation'

questions.question: Differentiate between Gane-Sarson and Yourdon notation for Data Flow Diagrams with respect to: (i) symbol representation for processes, (ii) data store representation, and (iii) external entity representation. Provide a comparative table showing the visual differences.
keyPoints: Gane-Sarson: rounded boxes for processes, open-ended box for data stores, square for entities
Yourdon: circle/bubble for processes, open rectangle with two lines for data stores, rectangle for entities
Gane-Sarson uses distinct shapes for all elements, Yourdon uses more minimal representation
Choice of notation impacts DFD readability and organizational standards

questions.question: Consider an Online Shopping System with the following requirements: Customers can browse products, add items to cart, place orders, and make payments. The system maintains product inventory and generates shipping requests. Draw a Context Diagram (Level-0), followed by Level-1 DFD showing at least 4 major processes (Browse Products, Manage Cart, Process Order, Handle Payment). Show all external entities, data stores, and data flows with proper numbering. Justify your decomposition approach.
keyPoints: Context diagram shows system as single process with external entities: Customer, Admin, Payment Gateway, Shipping Service
Level-1 processes: P1-Browse Products, P2-Manage Cart, P3-Process Order, P4-Handle Payment
Data stores: D1-Product Database, D2-Customer Database, D3-Order Database, D4-Inventory
All input/output data flows must balance with context diagram flows; use sequential numbering (1.1, 1.2, etc.)

questions.question: A software analyst has created the following DFD fragment for an Employee Payroll System: Process 'Calculate Salary' receives 'Employee Hours' and 'Pay Rate' from Data Store 'Employee Records' and outputs 'Salary Details'. The analyst numbered this process as '3.1.2' and created a data flow 'Pay Data' from this process to Data Store 'Payroll Database'. Evaluate this DFD fragment against DFD rules and conventions. Identify all errors and suggest corrections. Also explain how you would decompose this process further into Level-2 DFD showing sub-processes for deductions, taxes, and net pay calculation.
keyPoints: Process numbering issue: 3.1.2 implies parent process 3.1 exists at Level-1, which should be shown
Data stores cannot be directly connected without process - violates DFD rule
Input flows to process should be transformed, not just passed through
Level-2 decomposition: P3.1.1-Read Employee Data, P3.1.2-Calculate Gross Pay, P3.1.3-Process Deductions, P3.1.4-Calculate Tax, P3.1.5-Determine Net Pay
